w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: AI vs Real Human Image Detection System

Build a comprehensive forensic analysis system that accurately detects AI-generated human images through multi-modal technical analysis, providing explainable results to combat misinformation, identity fraud, and deepfake content.
The AI Image Authenticity Detector is a hybrid forensic system combining:
that adapts to evolving AI generation techniques

Modern AI image generation tools (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3, Midjourney, etc.) create photorealistic human images that are visually indistinguishable from real photographs. This technological advancement enables:
are easily bypassed by new AI models
can be easily forged or stripped
Combines 7 independent forensic modules + 1 AI classifier
Provides explainable, weighted fusion of evidence
Operates at pixel and frequency levels beyond human perception
Real images incorrectly flagged as AI
AI images incorrectly flagged as real
Quickly identify fake profiles using AI-generated images
Reduce time spent on manual verification
Improve accuracy of profile authenticity checks
Handle high volume of daily reports (500+ profiles/day)
Manual review is time-consuming and subjective
Difficult to keep up with evolving AI generation quality
Lacks technical tools for definitive verification
Faces pressure to minimize false positives (user complaints)
Fast, automated detection (<5 seconds per image)
Clear confidence scores to prioritize reviews
Explainable results to justify moderation decisions

Sarah receives a report of a suspicious profile. She uploads the profile picture to the detector, which returns an 87% confidence AI-generated verdict with reasons (no sensor noise, facial asymmetry). She removes the profile and documents the technical justification.
Persona 2: Digital Forensics Investigator - Michael Rodriguez
Role: Law Enforcement Digital Forensics Analyst
Experience: 15 years in forensics, 5 in digital evidence
Authenticate photographic evidence in criminal cases
Distinguish between edited and AI-generated images
Cannot rely on black-box AI without understanding methodology
Requires detailed technical reports for expert testimony
Detailed forensic analysis reports with scientific basis
Ability to explain findings to non-technical judges/juries

a suspected fake ID photo. The system provides a comprehensive report showing PRNU analysis (no camera fingerprint), FFT artifacts (diffusion model signature), and facial inconsistencies. He uses this multi-evidence report in court testimony, withstanding cross-examination by explaining each forensic technique.
Role: Senior Software Engineer at Dating App Startup
Integrate automated verification into user onboarding
Reduce fake profiles on the platform
Scale verification to handle 10,000+ daily signups
Needs easy API integration with minimal development overhead
Requires high uptime and reliability for production use
Must balance security with user experience
Concerned about API costs at scale
Well-documented RESTful API with code examples
SDKs for common programming languages (Python, Node.js, Java)

Priya integrates the API into the signup flow. When users upload a profile photo, it's sent to the detection endpoint. If flagged as AI-generated (>80% confidence), the user receives a message to upload a selfie video for additional verification. Legitimate photos are approved instantly, maintaining smooth UX.
Role: Investigative Journalist at Major News Organization
Experience: 10 years in journalism, 3 in fact-checking
Verify authenticity of images from social media sources
Limited technical expertise in image forensics
Needs quick verification without deep technical knowledge
Ability to generate shareable verification reports

David encounters a viral image claiming to show a public figure in a compromising situation. He uploads it to the web interface, which returns a clear verdict: "AI-Generated (92% confidence)" with plain-language reasoning. He cites the analysis in debunking the false claim.
Automatic resolution normalization to standard processing size
Safe image loading with validation (file type, size limits)
image, padding=0.2): Crop with context padding
All listed image formats load successfully
Face detection achieves >95% accuracy on frontal faces
Pixel-level noise preserved (verified via noise analysis)
Invalid/corrupted files handled gracefully with error messages
Verify noise preservation by comparing before/after preprocessing
Every camera sensor has a unique noise pattern (fingerprint) caused by manufacturing imperfections. Real photos contain this fingerprint; AI-generated images do not.
Extract sensor noise fingerprint from image
Apply high-pass filtering to isolate noise from content
Compare against expected sensor noise characteristics
Generate PRNU confidence score (0.0 = no sensor noise, 1.0 = strong sensor noise)
2. Apply high-pass filter (removes content, keeps noise)
5. Calculate entropy and randomness of residual
6. Compare to reference sensor noise patterns
Clear separation between real and AI distributions
Natural camera noise is random and chaotic. AI-generated images often have artificially smooth or patterned noise.
Detect artificial smoothness or repetitive patterns
Generate noise naturalness score (0.0 = artificial, 1.0 = natural)
AI generation models (especially diffusion models and GANs) leave characteristic frequency-domain signatures. Real camera images have smooth frequency decay.
Detect periodic artifacts (grid patterns, repetitive frequencies)
Generate frequency artifact score (0.0 = clean, 1.0 = artifacts detected)
6. Compare to expected natural frequency decay
Diffusion model artifacts detected with >75% accuracy
Real images show smooth frequency decay
Visualization of frequency spectrum available for debugging
In physically consistent lighting, all shadows should point away from a single light source. AI models sometimes generate inconsistent shadow directions.
Detect facial shadows (nose, cheek, chin, neck)
Verify consistency with single light source hypothesis
Generate shadow consistency score (0.0 = inconsistent, 1.0 = consistent)
2. Identify shadow regions using edge detection and intensity analysis
3. Calculate gradient directions in shadow areas
4. Estimate light source position from shadow vectors
5. Check consistency across multiple shadow locations
Works under various lighting conditions (front, side, top)
Gracefully handles images without clear shadows
May produce false positives in complex multi-light scenarios
Less reliable in evenly lit (studio) conditions
Requires clear facial shadows to be effective
Light reflections (highlights) in eyes and on skin should be consistent with a single light source. AI models may generate mismatched or physically impossible reflections.
Verify reflection positions are consistent with single light source
Check for mismatched reflection patterns between eyes
Generate reflection consistency score (0.0 = inconsistent, 1.0 = consistent)
2. Extract eye regions and detect bright spots (catchlights)
3. Compare catchlight positions between left and right eyes
5. Verify all reflections consistent with single light direction
Images without clear reflections (low light, matte skin)
AI models sometimes generate anatomically incorrect or asymmetric facial features that violate biological norms.
Flag suspicious perfect symmetry (uncommon in real faces)
Generate facial consistency score (0.0 = many inconsistencies, 1.0 = anatomically correct)
3. Compare to biological norms and variance
Real camera photos contain EXIF metadata (camera make/model, settings, timestamp). AI-generated images typically lack this or have generic software tags.
Extract EXIF data from image file
Generic software tags (Photoshop, GIMP without camera data)
Generate metadata authenticity score (0.0 = no camera data, 1.0 = complete camera data)
5. Detect common AI generation software signatures
Real camera images have complete metadata (score >0.7)
Metadata can be stripped from real images
Metadata can be forged (though difficult to fake all fields correctly)
This module provides supporting evidence, not definitive proof
A trained convolutional neural network learns to recognize subtle AI generation fingerprints that may not be captured by rule-based forensics.
Support detection of multiple AI generation models:
Generate AI probability score (0.0 = real, 1.0 = AI-generated)
Support model updates with new AI samples (continuous learning)
Base: ResNet50 or EfficientNet-B3 (pretrained on ImageNet)
Output Layer (1 unit, Sigmoid) → AI probability
Model achieves >85% accuracy on test set
Combines scores from all forensic modules using weighted fusion to produce final verdict and confidence score.
Accept scores from all 7 forensic modules + AI model
Support configurable weights for different use cases
Generate natural language explanation based on strongest indicators:
If PRNU < 0.3: "No camera sensor noise detected"
If Frequency > 0.7: "Frequency artifacts characteristic of AI generation"
If Facial < 0.4: "Facial inconsistencies or unnatural symmetry"
If AI Model > 0.8: "Strong AI fingerprint detected by neural network"
Weighted fusion improves accuracy vs single best method by >10%
Clear classification for >80% of images (not uncertain)
Reasoning includes top 3-5 most significant indicators
Confidence score correlates with actual accuracy (calibration)
- Batch upload: up to 10 images simultaneously
- Horizontal bar chart showing all 8 module scores
Side-by-side comparison with detected artifacts highlighted
Summary statistics (X% AI, Y% Real, Z% Uncertain)
Ability to filter and sort results
Intuitive interface requiring <3 clicks to get results
No technical jargon in default view (expert mode available)
API Key in header: Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY
"message": "Unsupported file type. Please upload JPEG, PNG, or
Get API usage statistics for authenticated user.
All endpoints fully functional and tested
SDKs provided for Python, Node.js, Java
- Generate professional PDF report for each analysis
- Include all scores, reasoning, and metadata
- Branded with system logo and version
Includes watermark: "Generated by AI Image Detector v1.0"
Support for data pipelines and analytics
- A/B test different fusion weight profiles
- Performance charts (accuracy, precision, recall over time)
Ability to retrain model with one click
- Log all image analyses for compliance and debugging
- Actual images: Deleted after 24 hours (unless user opts to save)
4. Preprocessing: Load → Validate → Normalize → Detect Face
5. Parallel execution of 7 forensic modules + AI model:
13. Delete image from temporary storage (24h later)
- GPU Instances: EC2 g4dn.xlarge (for AI model inference)
- Horizontal scaling: Add more API containers
- Separate compute for AI inference (GPU instances)
image_loader.py # Load image safely with validation
fusion/ # Score fusion and decision logic
metrics.py # Calculate accuracy, precision, recall, F1
│ # Run on test dataset, generate comprehensive report
- GPU acceleration for AI model inference
- Stateless API design for easy replication
- Load balancing across multiple API instances
- Separate scaling for compute-intensive modules (AI inference)
| Timeline | Daily Images | Concurrent Users | Infrastructure |
- Developer: API access with rate limits
- PII detection and masking in logs
- Regular security audits and penetration testing
- Graceful degradation: If one module fails, others continue
- Health checks and auto-restart for containers
- Disaster recovery plan with RTO <4 hours, RPO <1 hour
- Automatic retry logic (3 attempts for transient failures)
- Admin alerts for critical failures (email, Slack)
- Backward compatibility guarantee for v1 API
No training required for basic use
- Inline code comments for complex logic
- Plugin architecture for adding new modules
- Migration scripts for database schema changes
- Deprecated features marked 2 versions in advance
- Terraform scripts for infrastructure as code
- Ethnicities: Balanced representation (Asian, Black, Hispanic, White, etc.)
- Lighting conditions: Indoor, outdoor, studio, natural
- Compression levels: Uncompressed, light JPEG, heavy JPEG
1. Download from public datasets (Flickr CC, Kaggle)
3. Manual review to ensure real people
4. Face detection to filter out non-human images
1. Generate using various AI tools with diverse prompts
2. Collect from public AI image repositories
4. Label with source model and version
- Metadata: Source, model version, generation parameters
DVC (Data Version Control) for dataset versioning
Use only publicly available images with licenses
No PII (names, addresses) stored with images
Faces are not identifiable in training (diverse public datasets)
No training on user data without explicit consent
Opt-in program for users to contribute to improvement
Anonymization before storage (strip EXIF GPS, names)
"""PRNU score should be high for real camera image"""
"""PRNU score should be low for AI-generated image"""
"""Overly symmetric AI faces should score low"""
3. Upload batch of images → Verify all processed correctly
4. Invalid image format → Verify error handling
- Gradually increase load until system breaks
- Run system at 70% capacity for 24 hours
1. Upload profile picture and verify authenticity
5. Customize fusion weights for specific use case
- 80% of users successfully complete tasks without help
- Run on every code commit (CI/CD pipeline)
- Edge cases (very small/large images, corrupted files)
- Backward compatibility (API changes don't break existing clients)
Core forensic modules (PRNU, FFT, Facial, Pixel Noise)
Month 1: Setup + Data Collection + Core Modules
Month 2: AI Model Training + Fusion Engine
- Same as Phase 1 + 1 DevOps Engineer + 1 QA Engineer
Month 6: Final Testing + Documentation + Beta Launch
- Blog posts and social media campaign
Monthly model updates with new AI samples
AI models evolve faster than detection
Continuous dataset updates, modular architecture for easy retraining
Multi-modal approach, weight tuning, threshold optimization
Focus on accuracy and explainability, enterprise features
Proper licensing of datasets, patent review
Unable to detect latest AI models
Custom API for law enforcement databases
Right to know what data is collected
Opt-out of data selling (not applicable - we don't sell)
COPPA (Children's Online Privacy Protection Act) - No users under 13
HIPAA (if used in healthcare) - Not applicable initially
No attempts to reverse-engineer the model
Users should verify critical results independently
Use of MIT, Apache 2.0 licensed libraries (compliant)
Users retain ownership of uploaded images
We have license to process for service provision only
No commercial use of user images
Could be used to improve AI generators (adversarial training)
Mitigation: Limit API access, monitor for abuse
Clear communication that system is probabilistic
Encourage human review for critical decisions
Account creation and API key setup
Model training, forensic algorithms, accuracy optimization
Terms of Service, Privacy Policy review
An image created by an artificial intelligence model (e.g., Stable Diffusion, DALL-E) rather than captured by a camera.
A unique noise pattern inherent to every camera sensor, acting as a digital fingerprint.
Mathematical transformation that converts spatial domain image data into frequency domain, revealing periodic patterns.
A type of AI image generation model that starts with noise and iteratively refines it into a coherent image (e.g., Stable Diffusion).
A type of AI model consisting of a generator and discriminator that compete to create realistic images.
Exchangeable Image File Format data embedded in images, containing camera settings and other information.
Synthetic media created using AI, often depicting events or people in fabricated scenarios.
The reflection of light sources visible in a subject's eyes, useful for
Mirror-like reflection of light from shiny surfaces (e.g., skin, eyes).
Specific points on a face (e.g., eye corners, nose tip) used for analysis and recognition.
Apply high-pass filter to remove image content
Compare to expected sensor noise characteristics
Detect periodic peaks (indicative of AI artifacts)
Identify shadow regions using edge detection
Most competitors rely solely on deep learning models without explainability. We fill the gap with:
Marra, F., et al. (2019). "Do GANs Leave Specific Traces?" IEEE WIFS.
Lukas, J., et al. (2006). "Digital Camera Identification from Sensor Pattern Noise." IEEE Transactions.
Frank, J., et al. (2020). "Leveraging Frequency Analysis for Deep Fake Image Recognition." ICML.
Wang, S., et al. (2020). "CNN-generated Images Are Surprisingly Easy to Spot... For Now." CVPR.
Complete PRD with all sections, ready for review
This PRD is a living document and will be updated as the project evolves. All changes will be tracked in the change log (Appendix E).
